planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May. Tropicanna Gold produces the brightest, most vivid golden-yellow-striped foliage in the Tropicanna series — each leaf is green with prominent golden-yellow to cream-yellow stripes running the length of the leaf. The vivid orange flowers provide a complementary warm-toned floral display above the striped foliage. In zone 9 canna rhizomes can remain in the ground year-round as permanent perennials, forming spreading clumps that increase in size each season. Divide clumps every 2–3 years when they become crowded. Water stress and nutrient deficiency reduce foliage color intensity — maintain consistent moisture and fertilization for the best striped effect.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, consistently moist soil — Tropicanna Gold Canna produces the most vivid foliage color in full sun but performs well in partial shade in zone 9 where afternoon shade reduces the heat stress on the large foliage; Tropicanna Gold is a golden-striped foliage canna producing extraordinarily vivid green-and-gold variegated leaves — broad, paddle-shaped, tropical leaves with prominent longitudinal golden-yellow to cream-yellow stripes that create a boldly patterned tropical foliage effect unlike any other commonly grown garden plant. The combination of the large scale of canna foliage (leaves 12–18 inches wide on plants 4–5 feet tall) with the vivid yellow-striped pattern creates one of the most dramatic tropical foliage statements available in the summer landscape — immediately visible from a distance and creating a strong focal point in any garden composition. Above the spectacular striped foliage, Tropicanna Gold produces bright orange flowers in the classic canna form, which extend the ornamental display from late spring through frost in zone 9 and provide hummingbird nectar. In zone 9 canna is one of the most permanent and reliable tropical perennials available — surviving winter in the ground, re-emerging and producing increasingly large clumps each year, and creating a progressively more impressive foliage display as the planting matures.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Canna Varieties for Florida Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Perennials for Gulf South Gardens
American Horticultural Society — Canna x generalis Cultivar Guide

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost or plant transplants after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant transplants March–May. Sunstander is a sun-tolerant impatiens selection developed in response to the nearly complete collapse of Impatiens walleriana production caused by impatiens downy mildew (Plasmopara obducens), which devastated annual impatiens plantings across the Eastern US starting around 2012. The Sunstander series was selected for greater sun tolerance than standard walleriana — making it useful in partial shade and high-light situations where standard impatiens produce leggy, sparse growth. In zone 9 impatiens perform best as a spring and fall annual, declining in peak summer heat; plan for replacement or expect reduced flowering in July–August.

directions: Plant in partial shade to filtered light in well-drained, fertile, humus-rich, consistently moist soil — Sunstander impatiens tolerates more sun than standard Impatiens walleriana and performs well in morning sun with afternoon shade in zone 9; Sunstander is a sun-tolerant selection of Common Impatiens (Impatiens walleriana) developed to address the practical limitations of standard impatiens in higher-light situations — the standard selections requiring deep shade conditions that limit planting flexibility in many garden situations. Impatiens walleriana remains one of the most valuable shade-garden annuals available — producing a continuous, weed-suppressing carpet of single or double flowers in essentially every color except true blue, requiring almost no deadheading, and delivering consistent performance from planting through frost in shaded beds, window boxes, and containers. The Sunstander selection expands the practical planting range of walleriana impatiens by tolerating the dappled light and part-sun conditions that cause standard impatiens to become leggy and produce sparse flowers rather than the dense, mounding bloom mass they produce in appropriate shade. In zone 9 impatiens are most valued as spring and fall annuals — planted in March for spring display and replaced or supplemented in fall (September–October) when they recover from summer heat stress.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Impatiens walleriana for Florida Shade Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Annual Shade Flowers for Gulf South
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Sun-Tolerant Impatiens for Zone 9 Partial Shade

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall (October–November in zone 9); fall planting is essential for daffodils — they require a period of cool soil temperatures during winter for proper flower development. In zone 9 daffodils do not reliably perennialize due to insufficient winter chilling — treat as annual spring bulbs or purchase pre-chilled bulbs. Ice Follies is widely regarded as one of the most reliable and widely grown daffodil cultivars globally — its adaptability to a wide range of conditions including some heat-tolerance making it more successful in the Gulf South than most large-cupped daffodils. In zone 9 the pale cream to white flowers with the pale yellow cup that fades to cream as the flower ages appear January–February, providing one of the earliest spring flower displays. Allow foliage to die back naturally; do not braid or remove leaves before they yellow.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade — daffodils under deciduous trees receive adequate sun while the trees are leafless during their growing and blooming season — in well-drained, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly acidic soil with consistent moisture during the fall and winter growing season; Ice Follies is one of the most widely planted large-cupped daffodils in the world — a Division 2 large-cupped daffodil producing large (4-inch), clean, creamy-white flowers with a broad, flattened, shallow cup in pale primrose-yellow that fades to cream-white as the flower matures, creating a clean, all-white or cream-and-white effect at full maturity. The large flower size, vigorous growth, adaptability to a wide range of climates (zones 3–9), and reliability of bloom have made Ice Follies the standard by which large-cupped white daffodils are evaluated — it remains one of the best-selling and most widely planted named daffodil cultivars in commercial production after more than 60 years in the trade. The naturalizing tendency of Ice Follies — producing multiplying clumps that increase in flower count each year in appropriate climates — combined with the complete deer resistance of Narcissus bulbs and foliage make daffodils one of the most practical long-term spring bulb investments for zone 9 gardeners in cooler microclimates.
sources: American Daffodil Society — Narcissus 'Ice Follies' Registration and Description
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Daffodil Varieties for Gulf South Gardens
University of Georgia Extension — Spring Bulbs for Southern Climates

planting.note: Direct sow shelled peanuts (seed) after last frost when soil temperature is reliably above 65°F; in zone 9 sow March–May. Plant shell-on or shelled peanuts (not roasted) 1.5–2 inches deep; do not remove the seed coat. Georgia Green is the leading commercial Virginia-type peanut variety in the US, widely grown across the South for its disease resistance, high yield, and excellent flavor. Peanuts form their pods underground — after the yellow flower petals fall, the fertilized ovary elongates into a 'peg' that grows downward and pushes into the soil where the pod develops. In zone 9 dig peanuts when the inner pod shell shows the characteristic veining pattern and the pods have formed mature kernels (110–130 days from planting). Sandy, well-drained, calcium-rich soil is ideal — peanuts require calcium around the developing pods.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral, calcium-rich soil — peanuts require loose, deep, well-drained soil for the pegs to penetrate easily and the pods to develop properly; Georgia Green is the leading commercial and home garden Virginia-type peanut variety in the United States, released by the North Carolina State University breeding program and immediately adopted across the peanut belt for its superior resistance to late leaf spot (Cercosporidium personatum) and Tomato Spotted Wilt Virus (TSWV) — the two most economically important peanut diseases in the Southeast. The Virginia type produces larger kernels than Spanish or Valencia peanuts, with the characteristic elongated, two-kernel pods and excellent roasted flavor that make Virginia peanuts the preferred type for in-shell roasting, boiling, and gourmet peanut products. Georgia Green has maintained commercial adoption for over 25 years — an unusual longevity for a field crop variety — because the disease resistance package continues to provide practical advantages over older and newer alternatives under Gulf South conditions. In zone 9 the long, hot growing season (110–130 days) and sandy coastal plain soils are ideal for peanut production, making Georgia Green one of the most naturally adapted and rewarding warm-season food crops available for Gulf South gardens.
sources: North Carolina State University Extension — Georgia Green Peanut Variety Profile
University of Georgia Extension — Peanut Production for Georgia Home Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Peanut Production in Texas

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 sow April–July. Iron and Clay is a hybrid between iron cowpea and clay cowpea — producing a vigorous, drought-resistant, fast-covering warm-season cover crop that is one of the most widely planted summer cover crops in the Gulf South and Southeast US. It is primarily used for deer food plots, summer cover, erosion control on disturbed soils, and green manure preceding fall vegetable plantings. Inoculate seed with cowpea Rhizobium inoculant for maximum nitrogen fixation. The spreading vines can reach 18–24 inches tall and cover the ground rapidly, suppressing summer weeds effectively. Mow or disk and incorporate before seed set if naturalizing is not desired. Excellent summer deer forage — often planted in wildlife food plots.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed in full sun in any well-drained to moderately drained soil — Iron and Clay Pea adapts to poor, sandy, low-fertility soils where other cover crops struggle; inoculate with cowpea Rhizobium for maximum nitrogen fixation; Iron and Clay Pea is a hybrid cowpea (Vigna unguiculata) cover crop that is one of the most drought-tolerant, heat-adapted, rapid-covering warm-season green manures available for Gulf South summer use — producing a dense, weed-suppressing ground cover within 4–6 weeks of germination and fixing 100–200 pounds of nitrogen per acre per season when properly inoculated with Rhizobium. The exceptional heat and drought tolerance of Iron and Clay allows it to establish and cover ground through the peak of Gulf South summer (June–September) when soil temperatures, dry spells, and heat stress defeat other cover crops and leave bare soil exposed to erosion and weed pressure. As a wildlife food plot crop, Iron and Clay Pea is one of the most widely planted warm-season forages for white-tailed deer in the Southeast — the highly palatable vines and pods providing high-protein forage through summer and early fall. For zone 9 vegetable gardeners, Iron and Clay sown in June–July and incorporated in September–October provides both summer weed suppression and a substantial nitrogen boost for fall vegetable plantings.
sources: USDA NRCS — Iron and Clay Cowpea Cover Crop Specification
University of Georgia Extension — Warm-Season Cover Crops for Georgia
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Summer Cover Crops for Gulf South Soils
